summary: I was born and raised in Crisfield, Maryland, and I've lived here almost my entire life, except for a brief six-month stay in Pennsylvania. Growing up in the 70s and 80s, Crisfield was a bustling town with a strong economy centered around fishing and crabbing. Over the years, the town has slowed down considerably, with fewer businesses and less activity, but it's still home to me because of family and the deep connection I feel here. Despite the changes, I cherish the community events and the waterfront that define Crisfield.

My family and I moved away to Pennsylvania for about six months, but it was hard to stay away from Crisfield. Even though Pennsylvania was a different environment, I always felt drawn back to my hometown. That short time away made me appreciate Crisfield even more as my true home. It’s a place that sticks with you no matter where you go.

The Crab Derby was always one of my favorite events growing up in Crisfield. Every year, we looked forward to it as a highlight of the summer, along with other community celebrations like the Clam Bake and fireworks. Although the event has had some ups and downs, especially with COVID, it’s still around and even improved last year. It’s a big part of the town’s identity and brings people together.

The Clam Bake was one of the many community events we enjoyed in Crisfield during my youth. Alongside the Crab Derby, circuses, and carnivals, it made the holidays and summers really special. While some events like circuses and carnivals have faded away, the Clam Bake remains a fond memory of the lively social life we had back then. These gatherings helped create a strong sense of community.

I sometimes attend events at the Elks in Crisfield, though not regularly. It’s one of the few places where community gatherings still happen, along with the American Legion. At my age, I tend to keep a slower pace, but the Elks remains a spot where locals can come together for social events. It’s part of the fabric of community life here.

The American Legion is another organization in Crisfield where I sometimes go for events. Like the Elks, it offers a place for community members to gather, though I don’t attend often these days. It’s part of the social scene that still exists, even as the town has slowed down. These organizations help keep some community spirit alive.

The Chesapeake Bay is central to life and recreation in Crisfield. Without it, I don’t know what the town would do, since so much revolves around the water. People come here for canoeing, kayaking, and just to enjoy the marina and beach areas. The waterfront is a big draw for both locals and tourists, and it’s a defining feature of our community.

Hurricane Sandy was a major event that everyone in Crisfield remembers vividly. It caused widespread flooding and damage, with many buildings sitting dormant for a long time afterward. My parents were flooded in and had to do extensive repairs, and it was a scary time for the whole town. The rebuilding process took a long time, including remodeling this building, which reopened in July 2024 after 14 months.

I remember when the Maryland National Guard came to help during flooding back when I was in middle school. They brought my mother home safely when our apartment near Woodson School was flooded. That was a memorable moment that showed how important their role is during emergencies in Crisfield. Their assistance was crucial during severe weather events.

Woodson School is near where I lived in apartments that used to flood during heavy rains. I recall the flooding there vividly, especially the time when the National Guard had to bring my mother home. The school is part of the neighborhood that experiences these flood challenges. It’s a reminder of how weather impacts daily life in Crisfield.

TidalHealth bought a part of downtown Crisfield, which used to be the hub of the town. Since Hurricane Sandy, many buildings downtown have been left dormant, and the area looks pretty bad. The sale to TidalHealth is part of the changes happening here, but it’s unclear how much progress has been made to revitalize the area. It’s a sign of the challenges Crisfield faces in recovery and redevelopment.

Gordon's is a local spot where people talk about the weather, especially in winter. Folks there share stories about snow days from when we were younger, comparing them to the flood days we have now. It’s a place where community members reflect on how the climate has changed over the years. The conversations at Gordon’s show how much people notice the shifts in weather patterns here.
